notes: Chittim (Kittim). Early name for Cyprus, an island colonized and controlled by Tyre. Since Tyre will be laid waste, so that there will be no house or harbor for people or ships to rest in, Chittim is an ideal location for Tyre's refugees, who will bring news of the destruction to Tyre's merchants who are traveling away from home. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.206-207)

ships of Tarshish. The location of Tarshish is unknown. The ships of Tarshish symbolize Tyre's people, and especially the merchants who, because of their wealth and prestige, represent the world's pride and arrogance (see commentary on Isa. 2:16). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.206)

Tyre/Zidon (Sidon). Tyre and Sidon, Phoenicia's chief cities, are a symbol for the whole land of Phoenicia; like Babylon, they symbolize the world that will be judged by God (D&C 1:16). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.206)

Be still, ye inhabitants of the isle. Be still (Hebrew damam) carries two meanings associated with stillness, "motionless" and "silent."12 Isle specifically refers to the island of Tyre. In contrast to howl in 23:1, the Lord commands the inhabitants of Tyre to be still. The once bustling city of Tyre will be silenced by the destruction of her houses, port, and inhabitants. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.207)

merchants . . . replenished. Sidon's merchants carried corn, dyed cloth, grain, wine, metal, horses, wood, and oil as they voyaged on the seas. Tyre and Cyprus were supported (replenished) by Sidon's trading (see commentary on 2:6). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.207)

seed of Sihor/harvest of the river. Seed and harvest refer to the grain produced near Sihor (Josh. 13:3) and the Nile River and shipped by the Phoenicians over great waters to other nations, thereby providing Phoenicia with revenue. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.207)

market place of nations. During Isaiah's time, Tyre and Sidon were the Mediterranean's most powerful commercial cities; the Mediterranean nations all traded with Phoenicia. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.207)

ashamed. According to Israelite tradition, shame fell on the woman who was barren or violated, or who lost all her children through death. Tyre and Sidon, portrayed as women, will be humbled and shamed. Tyre, referred to as the "strength of the sea," will be virtually depopulated, her people killed, enslaved, or made refugees, and therefore unable to raise up a new generation of young men and virgins to populate the island. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.207)

young men/virgins. The lack of young men and virgins implies the loss of honor and life, symbolizing Tyre's destruction and the enslavement of her inhabitants. So will it be with the wicked in the last days. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.207)

I travail not, nor bring forth children, neither do I nourish up. Tyre, presented as a barren or vanquished woman, speaks of the shame she will feel when she becomes childless through the loss of sons and

daughters in battle. It will be as if Tyre had never been populated and the sea had never been a major shipping lane. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.207)

Egypt . . . will be sorely pained at the report. Egypt's inhabitants, who rely heavily on trade with Tyre, will be hurt by Tyre's destruction. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.207)

Pass ye over to Tarshish. Those who survive or escape the destruction of Tyre are instructed to flee to Tarshish, which had once brought them great profit. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)
howl, ye inhabitants. Tyre's inhabitants will mourn the loss of Tyre as they flee to Tarshish (23:1). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)

joyous city, whose antiquity is of ancient days. Tyre took pride and joy in her prosperity and antiquity, and Isaiah mocks her inhabitants with a question that essentially asks, "Where is your joyous, bustling city now?" (22:2; 32:13). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)

her own feet shall carry her afar off to sojourn. After the destruction of their city, Tyre's refugees will migrate to distant places. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)

merchants are princes/traders are the honorable of the earth. Tyre's merchants are so well-respected and famous, it is as if they were princes, or sons of kings. The honorable of the earth can be understood as those who receive the honors of men rather than those of God (see commentary on 47:15; see also 3:3-4). The scriptures make it clear that the honors of men are worthless in eternity because only those honors conferred by God will persist (Alma 60:36). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)

Who hath taken this counsel against Tyre. Isaiah answers his own question: The Lord, who made this plan against Tyre, hath taken this counsel. The Hebrew word ya'ats,14 translated as both "counsel" (23:8) and "purposed" (23:9), speaks both of the plans made and of the determination to follow through. Elsewhere in Isaiah the Lord plans the destruction of Babylon (14:24, 27) and Egypt (19:12). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)

crowning city. This description represents Tyre's splendor and glory-her pride. Despite her worldly greatness, the Lord of Hosts is about to bring her low (see Isa. 23:1). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)
The Hebrew word ya'ats, translated as both "counsel" (23:8) and "purposed" (23:9), speaks both of the plans made and of the determination to follow through.

defile the pride/bring into contempt. The Lord determines to bring down the proud of Tyre, causing her glory to cease; instead, she will be defiled. This same fate will fall upon the proud in the last days (see commentary on 2:12-17; Lev. 26:19; Prov. 16:18). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)

Cultivate thy land as along the Nile. Because Tyre's inhabitants have lost their domination of the trading market and been pressed into servitude by a foreign nation, they must resort to cultivating their land, as was the practice along the Nile River in Egypt. The occupation of merchant was preferred to farming-but the people of Tyre will be forced back to the land. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)

daughter of Tarshish. Tyre is essentially dependent on Tarshish ships for her strength and livelihood (see commentary on 23:1). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.208)
daughter of Zidon (Sidon). It was customary for Isaiah to call cities daughter (23:10; 37:22; 47:1). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.209)
oppressed virgin. Sidon is portrayed as a woman, as is customary for cities referred to by Isaiah. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.209)

this people did not exist. The Chaldeans were Semitic nomads who came to be known as Babylonians. They inhabited the wilderness areas of the Fertile Crescent where Babylon was built. They had little significance (did not exist) among the earth's nations until the Assyrians came and built cities and fortifications ("founded it") in their land, thus making the city of Babylon powerful. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.209)

they stripped its palaces. The Assyrians stripped Sidon's palaces of their ornaments, wealth, and leaders; they even pulled down Tyre's walls. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.209)

he brought it to a ruin. The subject here is the Lord, who brought about Tyre's harsh and swift ruin through Assyria (25:2). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.209)
set up the towers. The Assyrian army set up siege towers as they surrounded Tyre. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.209)

Howl, ye ships of Tarshish: for your fortress is laid waste. Isaiah reiterates the command to the Tarshish ships to howl (23:1). Again, this prophecy symbolizes the great destruction of the proud and the wicked at the end of the world; they will have ample cause to howl and mourn. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.209)

as the song of the harlot. This may be a street song heard in seaport towns, directed to a forgotten harlot who tries to revitalize her image and former occupation. The harlot is encouraged to take up her harp and sing "about the city" to gain attention. Isaiah uses this song to refer to Tyre and her profitable trade, which could be compared to prostitution because Tyre sought to satisfy the carnal desires of men by selling them all manner of wares. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson,

forgotten seventy years/days of one king. The decline of the Assyrian empire permitted Tyre to regain commercial domination (23:17) approximately seventy years after her destruction (see commentary on Isa. 23:1). The days of one king are said to be seventy years. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.210)

Contrasting the message of doom of the previous section, the Lord now reveals a message of restoration: After seventy years, Tyre will begin to rebuild her trading industry (23:16-17) and will sing "the song of the harlot" (23:15). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.210)
Tyre and Sidon Restored for the Purposes of the Lord (23:15-18)

go about the city/sing many songs, that thou mayest be remembered. The forgotten harlot (Tyre) must go about her city (the world's marketplaces) to solicit men's attention (Prov. 7:10-19) with alluring cries (songs) in the hope of somehow regaining her former status among the nations. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.211)

the Lord will visit Tyre. Tyre was previously visited in judgment by the Lord (23:9, 11), but after the completion of the seventy-year sentence, the Lord will again visit Tyre, this time to restore her to her former status. But she will again find success in trading with the world's nations and return to her harlotry. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.211)

Tyre . . . shall commit fornication. This perhaps speaks of the immoral practices, greed, or idolatry that Tyre will sell to the world's kingdoms. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.211)
kingdoms of the world. Tyre's influence will again extend throughout the known world. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.211)

Isaiah teaches us of the great apostasy, when earth's inhabitants made three major errors: they "transgressed the laws, changed the ordinance, [and broke] the everlasting covenant" (24:5). Because of this great apostasy from God's truth and his plan of happiness, God has wasted, turned upside down, emptied, and utterly spoiled the earth. Furthermore, the earth mourns, fades away, languishes, is defiled, and a curse devours it (24:1-6); because of the apostasy, God has scattered and burned the earth's inhabitants so that the few who remain do not experience happiness-"all joy is darkened" (24:11). Although this prophecy of Isaiah speaks of many apostasies that have occurred during the history of the earth, it speaks especially of the period preceding the restoration of the gospel-an apostasy that continues to this day among most of the world's people. In a revelation to the Prophet Joseph Smith, the Lord quoted Isa. 24:5: "For they have strayed from mine ordinances, and have broken mine everlasting covenant." The Lord then explained, "They seek not the Lord to establish his righteousness, but every man walketh in his own way, and after the image of his own god." Because of these things, and "knowing the calamity which should come upon the inhabitants of the earth," the Lord "called upon . . . Joseph Smith, Jun., and spake unto him from heaven, and gave him commandments" (D&C 1:15-17). The calamity to which the Lord refers comprises the second half of this section. The great cause of the destruction preceding the Second Coming is the wickedness that grows from apostasy. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.212-213)

The Apocalypse of Isaiah, chapters 24-27. 24-25 is prophetic. 26-27 is a song that is sung. In the Old Testament, songs are slightly different than what you picture in the hymnbook, songs are long discourses in which history is quite often related and exposed in a spiritual way. As soon as they crossed the Red Sea, the first thing they did Exodus 15, was compose the Song of Exodus. There were long discourses of what expositions of doctrine and history. The songs became quite important, because they really began to relay the history of the people and synthesize it into a future history also. Reviewing Isaiah 24 and 25, one sees a pattern that is very common in Isaiah's writings; Isaiah often delivers a pronouncement of serious warnings mingled with a note of optimism (as in chapter 24) and then follows it with a prophecy of joyful promises, concluding with a somber tone of caution (as in chapter 25).

her hire shall be holiness to the Lord. This may refer to an ancient custom by which the wealth of a city designated for destruction was consecrated to the Lord (Josh. 6:17, 19; Micah 4:13). Or perhaps the time of which Isaiah speaks is yet to come, and Tyre is a symbol of the wealthy nations of the world who, in the last days, will bring gifts, money, and honor to the Lord and his people (60:5-7; Ps. 45:12; Hag. 2:8). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.211)

it shall not be hoarded. Tyre possessed treasuries, and her inhabitants "heaped up silver as the dust, and fine gold as the mire of the streets" (Zech. 9:3). Isaiah prophesies that Tyre will not hoard her treasures, but "her merchandise shall be for them that dwell before the Lord" (23:18). (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.211)
